ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general 'milk tea' class (FOODON_03301099); no specific oolong milk tea or less-sugar variant exists. Closest parent match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0FKM covers tea-based beverages with milk; no oolong-specific or reduced-sugar sub-code exists in the published FoodEx2 catalogue. Closest parent match used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept for oolong milk tea with reduced sugar identified in the SNOMED CT food hierarchy.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of oolong milk tea (less sugar) is approximately 250 ml (250 g), equivalent to a regular-sized cup commonly sold at Singaporean bubble tea and speciality tea stalls.

nutrient_source.title: Oolong Milk Tea (Less Sugar) — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Oolong Milk Tea (Less Sugar), cr_id D08010074. All seven reported nutrients (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre) are taken verbatim from the HPB record at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat is estimated from the HPB milk-tea fat profile; monounsaturated, polyunsaturated, trans fat, and micronutrient fields not reported by HPB are set to -1.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of sweetened milk tea beverages — analogous estimate based on 'Tea with milk and sugar' (GI ~45) from the University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry exists for oolong milk tea (less sugar) in the University of Sydney GI Database. The closest approved analogous entry is 'Tea with milk and sugar' (GI approximately 45, low category). Oolong milk tea with reduced sugar is expected to have a similar or slightly lower GI due to lower sugar content and the presence of oolong tea polyphenols which may modestly attenuate postprandial glucose response. GI of 45 is used as a conservative analogous estimate. Glycemic load calculated as: GI (45) × available carbohydrate per serving (27.1 g per 250 g serving) / 100 = 12.2.

hypertension.key_factor: Added sugar content (5.19 g/100 g) exceeds the 5 g/100 g beverage sugar threshold; independently raises blood pressure risk per AHA 2018 guidelines despite low sodium (17 mg/100 g)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid this beverage due to its added sugar content exceeding the 5 g/100 g beverage threshold; request plain unsweetened oolong tea as a safe alternative, and if milk is desired, choose low-fat or skim milk.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage classification (5.19 g sugar/100 g); liquid sugar raises postprandial blood glucose rapidly and 'less sugar' variants remain classified as SSBs under ADA guidelines
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should avoid this beverage as it remains a sugar-sweetened beverage (5.19 g sugar/100 g); liquid sugars are absorbed rapidly and elevate postprandial glucose — plain unsweetened oolong tea is the recommended alternative.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Combined effect of added sugar (5.19 g/100 g) and saturated fat from milk (~1.52 g/100 g estimated); regular consumption of sugar-sweetened beverages is associated with increased CVD risk independent of other dietary factors
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit or avoid this beverage due to its added sugar and saturated fat content; oolong tea polyphenols offer modest antioxidant benefit but do not offset the cardiovascular risk from the sugar and fat load in this formulation.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Added sugar (5.19 g/100 g) promotes pro-inflammatory cytokine activity relevant to RA despite oolong tea polyphenol content
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this beverage as added sugar promotes inflammatory cytokines; plain unsweetened oolong tea without milk or sugar would be a suitable anti-inflammatory alternative.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Oolong Milk Tea (Less Sugar) is a popular Singaporean speciality tea beverage made by combining freshly brewed oolong tea with milk and a reduced quantity of added sugar. Oolong tea is a partially oxidised Chinese tea that sits between green and black tea in terms of oxidation level, imparting a distinctive floral, slightly roasted, and mildly sweet flavour profile. The addition of milk gives the beverage a creamy, smooth texture and a pale amber colour. The 'less sugar' designation indicates that the sugar level has been reduced compared to the standard preparation, typically to approximately 50–70% of the default sugar quantity used at Singaporean bubble tea and speciality tea stalls. Per 100 g, this beverage provides 66 kcal, 0.11 g protein, 2.47 g fat, 10.84 g carbohydrate, 5.19 g sugar, 17 mg sodium, and 1.37 g dietary fibre. Despite the reduced sugar label, the beverage still contains 5.19 g of sugar per 100 g, which classifies it as a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB) under ADA and AHA guidelines. This classification has direct clinical relevance: the beverage is unsuitable for individuals with type 2 diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease due to the rapid glycaemic impact of liquid sugar, the added sugar exceeding the 5 g/100 g beverage threshold for hypertension, and the combined sugar and saturated fat load relevant to cardiovascular risk. Oolong tea polyphenols (catechins, theaflavins) are present and may offer modest antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties, but these benefits are insufficient to offset the metabolic risks associated with the added sugar in this formulation. The beverage is commonly consumed at breakfast or as an afternoon drink in Singapore and is widely available at hawker centres, food courts, and speciality tea chains. It is vegetarian, halal-friendly, and gluten-free, but contains milk and is therefore not suitable for those with dairy allergies or lactose intolerance.

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre) are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database at confidence 0.95 for an exact named entry (cr_id D08010074). Saturated fat is estimated from the HPB milk-tea fat profile. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database. All other micronutrient fields not reported by HPB are set to -1 with documentation.
notes: Saturated fat (1.52 g/100 g) is estimated from the HPB milk-tea fat composition profile, as HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this entry; this value should be verified if a more detailed fatty acid breakdown becomes available. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1 because detailed micronutrient and fatty acid data for this specific oolong milk tea (less sugar) preparation are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or the USDA FoodData Central as of 2026. Protein (0.11 g/100 g) is a very low but non-zero value consistent with the minimal milk content in a tea-based beverage; this is not zero and requires no zero-macro justification. GI of 45 is an analogous estimate based on 'Tea with milk and sugar' from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for oolong milk tea (less sugar) was found in any approved source. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (45) × carbohydrate per 250 g serving (27.1 g) / 100 = 12.2. The 'less sugar' designation does not remove the SSB classification under ADA or AHA guidelines as sugar remains present at 5.19 g/100 g.
